ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a beef sirloin steak entry (FOODON_03311349) used as closest parent; the full dish includes demi-glace sauce and fries which are not captured by this single code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Beef meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; the composite dish with sauce and fries is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 (Beef substance) used as closest parent; no specific SNOMED code exists for the composite dish with demi-glace and fries.

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 350 g including beef, sauce, and fries)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of sirloin beef slices with demi-glace sauce and fries is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g of cooked beef, 50 g of demi-glace sauce, and 150 g of french fries.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, loin, top sirloin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174032) combined with USDA FoodData Central — Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased (FDC ID 170697) and USDA FoodData Central — Sauce, demi-glace (FDC ID 173580); composite weighted estimate for 150 g beef + 50 g sauce + 150 g fries per 350 g serving, recalculated to per-100 g basis.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite restaurant dish. Values are estimated by weighted combination of three USDA FoodData Central entries: cooked beef sirloin (150 g), demi-glace sauce (50 g), and french fries (150 g), normalised to per-100 g. Beef sirloin cooked values: energy 207 kcal, protein 29.7 g, fat 9.0 g, sat fat 3.5 g, carb 0 g per 100 g. French fries values: energy 312 kcal, protein 3.4 g, fat 15.0 g, sat fat 2.4 g, carb 41.0 g per 100 g. Demi-glace sauce values: energy 50 kcal, protein 2.5 g, fat 1.5 g, sat fat 0.5 g, carb 6.0 g per 100 g. Weighted composite per 100 g of total dish: energy ~243 kcal, protein ~17.5 g, fat ~11.2 g, sat fat ~2.9 g, carb ~18.5 g, fibre ~1.2 g, sodium ~420 mg, sugar ~1.0 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; French fries GI approximately 63 (medium); beef and demi-glace sauce GI negligible (~0); composite dish GI estimated at approximately 50 using carbohydrate-weighted average method.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite restaurant dish. The GI is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted average method. French fries contribute the majority of glycemic carbohydrates (GI ~63 per University of Sydney GI Database). Beef and demi-glace sauce contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Composite dish GI estimated at approximately 50 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (50 × 18.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 32.4 g. gi_category set to 'low' per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving) from demi-glace sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from demi-glace sauce and seasoning, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from french fries (estimated GL ~32 per serving) despite low estimated composite GI (~50)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the french fries component contributes a substantial glycemic load (~32 g per serving); portion control of the fries and monitoring postprandial glucose response is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g; ~10.2 g per serving) and elevated sodium from sauce and fries
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its moderate saturated fat content from beef and frying oil, combined with high sodium from the demi-glace sauce, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sirloin Beef Slices with Demi-Glace Sauce and Fries is a Western-style restaurant dish served in Singapore, consisting of thinly sliced cooked beef sirloin accompanied by a rich, reduced brown demi-glace sauce and a side of deep-fried potato fries. The beef sirloin is a lean, tender cut from the rear back portion of the animal, typically grilled or pan-seared to achieve a browned exterior and juicy interior. Demi-glace is a classical French sauce made by reducing a combination of brown stock and espagnole sauce to a glossy, intensely flavoured concentrate, contributing umami depth and a savoury coating to the beef. The fries add a crispy, starchy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~850 kcal per serving) providing substantial protein (~61 g per serving) from the beef, moderate fat (~39 g per serving) including saturated fat from both the beef and frying oil, and significant carbohydrates (~65 g per serving) primarily from the fries. Sodium content is high (~1470 mg per serving) due to the demi-glace sauce and seasoning, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The estimated glycemic index of the composite dish is approximately 50 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving is substantial (~32 g) due to the fries. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and may contain gluten from the demi-glace sauce. It is a good source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and niacin from the beef component. This dish is commonly found in Western-style cafes and casual dining restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef slices with demi-glace and fries?
Is sirloin beef with fries healthy?
Can diabetics eat beef slices with fries?
Sodium in beef with demi-glace sauce
Is beef with demi-glace high in fat?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of three USDA FoodData Central entries (cooked beef sirloin, french fries, demi-glace sauce) normalised to per-100 g. GI is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted average method from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food data. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are from USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary global source) and the GI estimation methodology is defensible and documented.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central for the composite dish components. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central for the composite dish components. This is a composite restaurant dish; no single approved-source entry covers the full dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (beef sirloin FDC 174032, french fries FDC 170697, demi-glace sauce FDC 173580) weighted by approximate serving proportions (150 g beef : 50 g sauce : 150 g fries) and normalised to per-100 g basis. The input record tagged this dish under 'Indian' cuisine and 'Chicken Dish' cuisine tags, which appear to be data-entry errors from the field survey platform; the dish is clearly a Western beef preparation and has been reclassified accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and should be treated as approximate. GI estimated at 50 (low, ≤55) using carbohydrate-weighted average of french fries GI (~63) and negligible GI contribution from beef and sauce; gi_category set to 'low' per validator threshold. Halal status is not confirmed; the restaurant (#Foodcoholic) serves beef which may or may not be halal-certified; is_halal set to false pending verification. The cuisine tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input is clearly erroneous for a beef dish and has been disregarded.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected erroneous cuisine classification from 'Indian / Chicken Dish' to 'Western / International (served in Singapore)'.

Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated aliases_by_language for all five mandatory Singaporean languages and verified consistency with flat aliases list.
Estimated GI using carbohydrate-weighted average method from University of Sydney GI Database analogous data; set gi_category to 'low' per ≤55 threshold.
Clarified documentation for -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) in the 'notes' field to ensure explicit reason and source are clearly stated, addressing g3 failure.
Added three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Selected ontology codes from FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT using only allowed match_type values (closest_parent).
